name: Ares
title: Greek - Ares

Ἀρὴς ἀνδροφόνος (Arēs androphonos) - Ares, slayer of men

primarySources.title: The Iliad
primarySources.author: Homer
primarySources.text: Homer's Iliad portrays Ares as a brutal and often ineffective god of war, frequently defeated and scorned by other deities, particularly Athena. His interventions in the Trojan War are driven by bloodlust rather than strategic acumen.

primarySources.title: The Odyssey
primarySources.author: Homer
primarySources.text: The Odyssey recounts the affair between Ares and Aphrodite, exposed by Hephaestus, highlighting Ares' impulsiveness and lack of cunning.

primarySources.title: Theogony
primarySources.author: Hesiod
primarySources.text: Hesiod's Theogony establishes Ares as the son of Zeus and Hera, though their relationship is often strained due to Ares' violent nature.

primarySources.title: Bibliotheca
primarySources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
primarySources.text: The Bibliotheca provides a comprehensive overview of Greek mythology, including various myths involving Ares, such as his conflicts with Heracles and his role in the founding of Thebes.

primarySources.title: Metamorphoses
primarySources.author: Ovid
primarySources.text: Ovid's Metamorphoses, while a Roman source, reflects the later interpretations of Ares (Mars) and his involvement in various mythological events, often focusing on his relationship with Venus (Aphrodite).

description:
Ares, the Greek god of war, stands as a complex and often controversial figure within the Olympian pantheon. Unlike Athena, the goddess of strategic warfare and wisdom, Ares embodies the raw, brutal, and untamed aspects of conflict. His domain encompasses the chaos, violence, and bloodshed of battle, making him a figure both revered and reviled by the ancient Greeks. While courage is associated with him, it is often depicted as reckless abandon rather than calculated bravery. His Roman counterpart is Mars, who enjoyed a far more prominent and respected position in Roman society.

Ares' parentage is attributed to Zeus, the king of the gods, and Hera, the queen of the gods, although their relationship with him is often strained. Hera, in particular, seems to harbor a certain disdain for her son's violent tendencies. Hesiod's *Theogony* establishes this lineage, solidifying his place among the primary Olympian deities. His siblings, depending on the source, include Hephaestus, the god of the forge, Hebe, the goddess of youth, and Eileithyia, the goddess of childbirth. However, Ares' temperament often sets him apart from his siblings, fostering a sense of isolation within the divine family.

In the *Iliad*, Homer portrays Ares as a figure driven by bloodlust and a thirst for battle. He frequently intervenes in the Trojan War, often siding with the Trojans, and is depicted as a powerful but ultimately ineffective warrior. He is often wounded and defeated by other gods, particularly Athena, who represents the strategic and civilized aspects of warfare. In one notable passage, Diomedes, aided by Athena, wounds Ares, forcing him to retreat from the battlefield in pain. This incident highlights Ares' vulnerability and lack of strategic prowess compared to Athena. Homer’s portrayal contributes to the nuanced understanding of Ares, showing him to be a force of destruction but also capable of being overcome.

Ares' relationship with Aphrodite, the goddess of love and beauty, is one of the most well-known aspects of his mythology. Their affair, as recounted in Homer's *Odyssey*, is a tale of passion and betrayal. Aphrodite is married to Hephaestus, the god of the forge, who is often depicted as physically unattractive and socially awkward. Ares, with his imposing physique and reputation for strength, represents the antithesis of Hephaestus. Their clandestine meetings are eventually discovered by Hephaestus, who crafts an intricate net to ensnare the lovers in their act of adultery. He then invites the other gods to witness their humiliation, exposing their affair to the entire Olympian pantheon. This episode reveals Ares' impulsiveness and lack of discretion, further contributing to his negative image. Their union resulted in several offspring, including Harmonia, the goddess of harmony, and, according to some traditions, Eros, the god of love (although Eros is more commonly attributed to Aphrodite alone).

Ares' children often reflect his violent and chaotic nature. Phobos (Fear) and Deimos (Terror) are his constant companions, accompanying him into battle and embodying the psychological effects of war. Eris (Discord), another frequent companion, stirs up strife and conflict wherever she goes. The Amazons, a tribe of warrior women, are also traditionally considered to be daughters of Ares, inheriting his martial prowess and independent spirit. These offspring reinforce the association of Ares with the negative aspects of war and conflict.

Several myths depict Ares in conflict with mortals and other gods. One notable story involves his conflict with Heracles (Hercules in Roman mythology). When Heracles sought to avenge the theft of some of his cattle, he encountered Ares' son, Cycnus, and killed him. Ares, enraged by the death of his son, challenged Heracles to a duel. However, Heracles, with the aid of Athena, defeated Ares, further diminishing his reputation. Another myth recounts Ares' imprisonment by the Aloadae giants, Otus and Ephialtes. The giants bound Ares in bronze chains and held him captive for thirteen months until Hermes rescued him. These stories highlight Ares' vulnerability and demonstrate that even a god of war can be overcome.

Despite his negative portrayal in many myths, Ares was also associated with certain positive qualities, such as courage and physical strength. He was revered by warriors and soldiers who sought his favor in battle. However, his worship was less widespread than that of other Olympian deities, and he lacked a significant cultural or political role in most Greek city-states. Sparta, known for its militaristic society, was one of the few places where Ares was particularly honored. The Spartans valued his martial prowess and considered him a patron deity of their military endeavors.

The symbols associated with Ares reflect his role as the god of war. The spear and sword are his primary weapons, representing his aggressive and combative nature. The shield and helmet symbolize his protection in battle. The chariot, often drawn by fire-breathing horses, signifies his swift and unstoppable advance. Animals such as the vulture and the dog, both scavengers associated with battlefields, are also linked to Ares, representing the aftermath of war and the cycle of violence. While some sources mention the serpent, barn owl, and woodpecker as being associated with Ares, these connections are less consistent and may vary depending on the region and time period.

Culturally, Ares embodies the darker aspects of human nature and the destructive potential of conflict. His portrayal serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked aggression and the importance of strategic thinking in warfare. While courage is a virtue, Ares' reckless abandon highlights the need for discipline and restraint. His frequent defeats at the hands of Athena underscore the superiority of intellect and strategy over brute force. The Greeks, while acknowledging the necessity of war, recognized its inherent brutality and sought to temper its destructive potential through reason and civilization. Ares, in his embodiment of raw violence, serves as a constant reminder of the price of conflict and the importance of seeking peaceful solutions.

Furthermore, Ares' character provides insight into the Greek understanding of the divine realm. The gods, while powerful and immortal, are not necessarily virtuous or benevolent. They are subject to the same passions and flaws as humans, and their actions often have unintended consequences. Ares, with his impulsiveness and lack of self-control, exemplifies this aspect of the Greek pantheon. His presence serves to humanize the gods and to make them more relatable to mortals.

The legacy of Ares extends beyond ancient Greece. His Roman counterpart, Mars, played a far more significant role in Roman society. Mars was not only the god of war but also a symbol of Roman power and military prowess. He was considered the father of Romulus and Remus, the founders of Rome, and was honored with numerous temples and festivals. The planet Mars is named after him, reflecting his association with warfare and aggression. In modern times, the figure of Ares continues to appear in literature, film, and popular culture, often as a symbol of conflict and violence. However, his portrayal is often more nuanced, exploring the psychological effects of war and the complexities of human nature. He remains a powerful and enduring figure in the collective imagination, representing the enduring fascination with the themes of war, courage, and destruction.

In conclusion, Ares, the Greek god of war, is a multifaceted and complex figure whose portrayal in mythology reflects the ancient Greek understanding of conflict and its consequences. While he embodies the raw power and violence of war, he also serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked aggression and the importance of strategic thinking. His relationships with other gods and mortals, as well as the symbols associated with him, further contribute to his rich and enduring legacy. He stands as a stark reminder of the human capacity for both creation and destruction, and the enduring need for balance and restraint.

Further academic research could explore the evolution of Ares' character across different periods of Greek history and literature. Examining the role of Ares in specific cults and rituals could provide additional insights into his religious significance. Comparing Ares to other war gods in different cultures could reveal broader patterns in the human understanding of conflict. Finally, analyzing the modern interpretations of Ares in popular culture could shed light on the enduring relevance of his mythology.

typologicalConnections.observation: Ares as god of war represents divine sanction for violence, which biblical tradition addresses through complex just war and holy war traditions.
typologicalConnections.distinction: The biblical God uses war instrumentally for judgment; Ares embodies war itself. Christ is 'Prince of Peace' (Isaiah 9:6); ultimate Christian hope is swords beaten into plowshares (Isaiah 2:4).

patristicCommentary.author: Augustine of Hippo
patristicCommentary.commentary: Augustine critiqued Roman glorification of war (Mars/Ares) while developing Christian just war theory that subordinates violence to justice and peace.

scholarlyEnrichment.methodology: Comparative religion from Christian scholarly perspective, incorporating patristic sources and typological analysis

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices of Animals
worshipPractices.description: Ares was propitiated through the sacrifice of animals, particularly bulls, dogs, and boars, which were seen as aggressive and warlike creatures. These sacrifices aimed to appease his violent nature and secure his favor in battle. Altars dedicated to Ares were often located in temples or near battlefields.

worshipPractices.name: Votive Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Warriors and soldiers would dedicate weapons, armor, and spoils of war to Ares as votive offerings. These offerings served as a tangible expression of gratitude for victory or as a plea for assistance in future conflicts. Such offerings symbolized a warrior's dedication and connection to the god of war.

festivals.name: Areia
festivals.timing: Specific dates are largely unknown but presumed to be seasonal, linked to military campaigns.
festivals.description: The Areia were festivals dedicated to Ares, primarily celebrated in regions with strong military traditions, such as Sparta. These festivals likely involved athletic competitions, military parades, and ritualistic combat to honor Ares's martial prowess and strength. Details of the specific rituals and celebrations are scarce.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ares's depiction as the embodiment of raw aggression continues to influence modern interpretations of war in literature and film. He often represents the darker, more destructive aspects of conflict, serving as a cautionary figure against unchecked violence. The god's name and associated imagery are also frequently used in military contexts and video games.

associations.name: Boar
associations.significance: The boar, known for its ferocity and aggression, mirrors Ares's untamed and brutal nature in battle.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents the destructive and uncontrollable force of war that Ares embodies.

associations.name: Red
associations.significance: Red symbolizes blood, violence, and the rage associated with Ares and warfare.

associations.name: Conflict
associations.significance: Ares is the embodiment of conflict, representing the raw and untamed aspects of war.

associations.name: Mars
associations.significance: Mars, named after the Roman equivalent of Ares, is associated with war and aggression.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: Represents the duality of conflict, often involving two opposing sides, a concept central to Ares's domain.

associations.name: Thorn Bush
associations.significance: The thorn bush symbolizes the pain, difficulty, and harm inflicted during warfare, reflecting Ares's association with violent conflict.

companions.name: Phobos
companions.description: Phobos is the personification of fear and panic, often accompanying Ares into battle. He represents the terror associated with war.

companions.name: Deimos
companions.description: Deimos is the personification of dread and terror, similar to Phobos, and also a constant companion of Ares. He embodies the dread and horror of war.

companions.name: Enyo
companions.description: Enyo is a goddess of war and bloodshed, often associated with Ares and accompanying him in battle. She delights in the carnage of war.

companions.name: Cycnus
companions.description: Cycnus was a son of Ares who was known for his brutality and violence. He challenged Heracles and was ultimately defeated.

companions.name: Alectrona
companions.description: Alectrona is a daughter of Ares and Aphrodite. She is the goddess of the morning or sunrise.
